content.problemStatement: Given a insertionSort function that accepts an array as a parameter, sort the array using the **Insertion sort** algorithm.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array, and the second line contains N space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output the N integers of the sorted array in non‑decreasing order on a single line, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The single test case contains 8 numbers. After applying insertion sort the numbers are ordered increasingly.
The array is already sorted; insertion sort leaves it unchanged.
Insertion sort reverses the descending order into ascending order.

solution.approach: We use the classic insertion sort algorithm. Starting from the second element we keep the current element (key) and shift all larger elements of the sorted prefix one position to the right until the correct position for the key is found. The array is sorted in‑place, so no extra memory is required. This algorithm is simple, works well for the given constraints (N ≤ 1000) and matches the problem statement which explicitly asks for insertion sort.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case, which is acceptable for N ≤ 1000 and T ≤ 10.
